There's an element that can go right in here, roughly just above c:plotArea, called c:autoTitleDeleted (a boolean I believe). There are some examples on this analysis page that have it True (1), I usually see it False (0) when I come across it for some reason. (It probably makes sense to find the c:chart schema definition on another analysis page and paste it into the schema excerpt so we can see where the two fit with respect to each other. This one has it: https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/docs/dev/analysis/features/cht-chart.rst)

Anyway, we should figure out exactly what it does. The spec might have something to say about it:
https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/spec/ISO-IEC-29500-1/ISO-IEC-29500-1.pdf

My suspicion is that it overrides a title definition, like a visible/not visible flag. Assuming that's so, we'll need to deal with it. A couple design issues come to mind:

* Should there be (and does the MS API have) a separate property for Title.visible = True/False?
* When True is assigned to Axis.has_title, should we clear this flag if it's set?
* When False is assigned to Axis.has_title, where a title is defined (c:title element is present), should we delete the c:title element or just set c:autoTitleDeleted to True?

We'll probably also need to validate the PowerPoint behavior that when c:autoTitleDeleted is set True when a title is defined, that in fact no title appears (and it behaves exactly like when no title is defined as far as spacing and so forth).

The simplest scenario would be that it's just effectively redundant with the presence of a c:title element and we pretty much just delete it whenever we encounter it; but I suspect it's there for a reason we haven't discovered yet :)

I do note that there is a c:strRef inside c:tx, an alternative to c:rich, meaning the value can be defined in the Excel chart data (basically a worksheet cell address). That relationship would be lost if the c:title element was deleted, indicating to me there's a useful case for "hiding" the title rather than having to delete it if you don't want to show it just now.

Over the weekend I happened to be experimenting with Chart title a bit. I noticed that if you add a chart title, put some text in it, and then select No Title (all this in PowerPoint), then if you select Chart Title again, your text is gone. I believe I checked the XML with opc-diag and saw that the title element was actually deleted in that process. Anyway, we'll need to suss that out :)


@marcoabreu I figured out why it succeed in linux but fails in Windows with the same seed. It is simply `shuffle` uses different implementations for linux and Windows. For linux it uses GNU's parallel version of `std::random_shuffle` and for windows it uses sequential `std::shuffle`. The two are based on totally different algorithms.

We clearly have different views on this, and it's your library, so I'll stop after this comment. 
 I think the overall goal should be to make the library as easy to use and contribute to as possible.  Using non-standard python things or inconsistency with the rest of the deep learning world hinders both goals.

In tensorflow, LSTM is always used in the public facing APIs even as part of longer strings (https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/contrib/rnn).

At lower levels, they aren't consistent -- e.g. one of those test files mixes both `LSTM` and `Lstm`  (https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/14f564990e7548b4a5c41da0fc533ec5a7a25abe/tensorflow/contrib/rnn/python/kernel_tests/rnn_cell_test.py#L173).

Python adopts the full upper case for acronyms in the standard library, e.g.

https://docs.python.org/3/library/urllib.request.html#urllib.request.HTTPDefaultErrorHandler
https://docs.python.org/3/library/xmlrpc.server.html#xmlrpc.server.SimpleXMLRPCServer

Great test and good coverage. However, it is helpful to split this test method into smaller one. There are a few benefits:
1) Test failure is more accurate when test body is small. It renders more accurate test report.
2) A large test will stop execute whenever any assertion fails, therefore, the following test logic is skipped. It then runs fewer tests when test logic are distributed among smaller tests.

Overall, a unit test should contain only three stages:
1) Arrange
2) Act
3) Assert

To move a file to a parent directory:
 1. Select checkbox on file.
 2. Press Rename. The textbox is pre-populated with just the filename + extension (e.g. "my_notebook.pynb")
 3. Prepend `../` to the name (e.g. changing it to "../my_notebook.pynb")
 4. Enter.

I'm totally focused on the goal, this why, if the point is to make it incrementally, I dont understand why you prefer to have something which create a coupling between two things that can be totally decoupled HTTP and introduce an already identified issue. And, as you said, it's to handle 2 APIs, so maybe a generic solution with both HTTP and applicative cache is a little bit over-engineered.

I'm so focused I took the time to deeply details my review and my responses.

To me it's like saying: yes we have a performance issue so let's not resolve it but instead let's try to hide it by adding another issue on top of it.

Why not make the HTTP caching part as one step and use the provided and tested `cache.memoize` to cache the data as an another step ? (the signal part of this PR to invalidate cache is still valid with `cache.memoize`) ? If it's not doing the job, yes, we can incrementally raise the complexity to find another solution.

So, to stay focused, what I'm saying is that to solve this performance issue, we have 3 tools that needs to be handled separately:
- HTTP cache headers (which can be solved by this decorator and can benefit all APIs than need  to and can be cached)
- applicative cache, (which is already provided by Flask-Caching and can already be used with `cache.memoize` and for which the invalidation pattern is this PR should work)
- identify the real bottleneck source of this performance issue (which might be an interesting point to solve as it's the real issue)

This PR try to solve the 2 first point together and I don't think this is a good idea.
So, just so I'm clear, I'm not saying that they shouldn't be solved, I like the idea of having proper HTTP cache handling as well as having more efficient applicative cache use. I just think this is something that need to be splitted because it's 2 distinct problems with 2 distinct solutions.
